---
title: "Making Swiss HEI data reviewable through dashboards"
subtitle: "The Swiss HEI Open Data Dashboard turns institutional identifiers, research-assessment commitments, and data provenance into a shared review surface."
date: "2026-06-16"
lang: en-UK
author:
  - name: "Dr Moritz Mähr"
    orcid: "0000-0002-1367-1618"
    affiliation: "ETH Zurich"
description: "NAIF's dashboards make Swiss higher education data easier to inspect, question, and correct, from institutional identifiers to DORA and CoARA status."
other-links:
  - text: "Swiss HEI Open Data Dashboard"
    href: "../../dashboards/swiss-hei-open-data/"
  - text: "HEI and NAIF data changelog"
    href: "../../dashboards/data-changelog.html"
categories:
  - "General"
tags:
  - "Dashboards"
  - "Open data"
  - "Metadata quality"
proofread: false
image: "images/swiss-hei-open-data-dashboard.png"
image-alt: "Screenshot of the Swiss HEI Open Data Dashboard with identifier coverage table"
image-caption: "Swiss HEI Open Data Dashboard on the NAIF website. Rights: ETH Library / NAIF."
toc: true
draft: false
---

## Why this dashboard exists

NAIF's work on interoperability starts with a simple but demanding question: do we have a shared, inspectable, and maintainable list of Swiss Higher Education Institutions (HEIs) that can be linked reliably across systems? Without that baseline, discussions about repositories, research assessment, open scholarly metadata, and national aggregation quickly become fragile. The same institution may appear under several names, different abbreviations, different language variants, or different registry identifiers. Conversely, a single registry entry can sometimes hide mergers, sub-units, historical names, or affiliation structures that matter for interpretation.

The [Swiss HEI Open Data Dashboard](../../dashboards/swiss-hei-open-data/) was created to make that baseline visible. It currently covers **55 Swiss HEIs** and brings together names, short names, institution types, towns, and identifiers from sources such as [swissuniversities](https://www.swissuniversities.ch/en/topics/studying/accredited-swiss-higher-education-institutions), [ROR](https://ror.org/), [OpenAlex](https://openalex.org/), [Wikidata](https://www.wikidata.org/), [ISNI](https://isni.org/), [Crossref](https://www.crossref.org/), [GeoNames](https://www.geonames.org/), and OpenOrgs. The dashboard is therefore not just a table of links. It is a public review surface for the organisational metadata on which other analyses depend.

The current coverage is encouraging, but also uneven. In the shared baseline, 54 of 55 institutions have a ROR identifier, 53 have OpenAlex identifiers, and all 55 have a Wikidata identifier. ISNI coverage is lower, with 47 institutions covered, while Crossref Funder identifiers are present for only 13 institutions. OpenOrgs mappings are available for 45 institutions. These numbers should not be read as a ranking of registries or institutions. They show where different systems have different scopes, curation histories, and modelling assumptions. For NAIF, the important point is that these gaps and mismatches become visible enough to discuss and correct.

This matters because institutional metadata is connective tissue. It links repositories to research information systems, funder databases, publication indexes, authority files, research assessment frameworks, and public reporting tools. If institutional identifiers are missing or inconsistent, publications may be hard to attribute, affiliations may fragment across aliases, open access statistics may become less reliable, and comparisons between institutions may become difficult to interpret. A dashboard cannot solve these issues by itself, but it can make them concrete and reviewable.

## What the data shows and what it cannot settle

The [DORA & CoARA in Swiss Higher Education dashboard](../../dashboards/naif/) uses the same HEI baseline for another purpose: tracking Swiss HEI engagement with responsible research assessment frameworks. It currently records 24 DORA signatories, 16 CoARA signatories, and 12 CoARA members among the 55 institutions in the shared dataset. The visual focus is different from the Swiss HEI Open Data Dashboard, but the dependency is the same. To interpret DORA and CoARA adoption responsibly, we first need to know which institutions are in scope, how they are named, how they are classified, and whether status changes are captured consistently.

This is where the dashboards become more than publication outputs. They expose the assumptions behind the numbers. Should a specialised institution be counted separately or as part of a larger structure? Which source should be followed when institutional names differ between a national list, a registry, and the institution's own website? How should multilingual names be maintained? Which identifier should be treated as canonical when OpenAlex, ROR, Wikidata, ISNI, Crossref, GeoNames, OpenOrgs, and swissuniversities do not line up neatly? When an institution signs DORA or joins CoARA, how quickly should that status appear in a national dashboard, and who is best placed to verify it?

The [TOBI dashboard](../../dashboards/tobi/) adds another perspective. TOBI, *Towards Open Bibliometric Indicators*, investigated the findability and publication-output coverage of Swiss HEIs in Dimensions, Scopus, Web of Science, OpenAIRE, and OpenAlex. Its dashboard remains a project snapshot, with retrieval dates from the original TOBI work rather than a continuously updated institutional baseline. That distinction is important. TOBI helps show why identifiers and institutional matching matter for bibliometric visibility, while NAIF now extends the conversation towards shared maintenance, interoperability, and practical coordination. For more background, see the related post [How NAIF builds on TOBI's results in Switzerland](../../posts/2026-03-25-naif-continues-tobi/).

The [HEI and NAIF data changelog](../../dashboards/data-changelog.html) was added because the data itself has a history. The shared `hei.csv` did not appear fully settled. It was expanded, corrected, moved to a shared location, stripped of dashboard-specific columns, updated with additional identifiers, and adjusted after review. The changelog records row-count changes, restored and removed institutions, corrected status flags, refreshed OpenOrgs and OpenAIRE links, missing Wikidata and location fields, terminology changes, and display-name corrections. Some entries are reconstructed from git history, and the page says so explicitly. That is a feature, not a weakness: provenance is part of the data.

Several questions arise from this history. What is the right national scope for a Swiss HEI baseline used in interoperability work? How should the baseline relate to the swissuniversities list of accredited institutions, especially where public registries lag behind institutional changes? Which corrections should be made locally in NAIF, and which should instead be pushed upstream to ROR, Wikidata, OpenAlex, ISNI, Crossref, GeoNames, or other source systems? How should we distinguish between a harmless missing identifier and a gap that materially affects discoverability, attribution, or responsible research assessment? How can we avoid turning a project-maintained spreadsheet into yet another isolated authority file?

These questions do not have purely technical answers. They involve institutional knowledge, source governance, multilingual naming practice, and decisions about maintenance responsibility. The dashboards are useful precisely because they make these dependencies visible. They allow a repository manager, research information specialist, librarian, data steward, policy officer, or institutional representative to inspect the same row and ask: is this still correct?

## Review your institution's data

We invite Swiss HEIs and other stakeholders to review the information shown in the dashboards. Please check whether your institution's official English name, short name, institution type, town, multilingual labels, swissuniversities entry, ROR, OpenAlex, Wikidata, ISNI, Crossref Funder, GeoNames, and OpenOrgs identifiers are correct. For the NAIF dashboard, please also check whether DORA signatory status, CoARA signatory status, and CoARA membership are represented accurately.

Corrections can be sent to [naif@library.ethz.ch](mailto:naif@library.ethz.ch) or submitted through the [NAIF GitHub issue tracker](https://github.com/eth-library/naif/issues/new/choose). If a discrepancy originates in an external registry, we are also interested in coordinating source-level corrections where possible. Local dashboard fixes are useful, but durable interoperability improves most when the upstream records become better too.

The aim is not to freeze one perfect list. Swiss higher education changes, registries evolve, and assessment frameworks develop. The aim is to make the data visible enough that it can be questioned, updated, and reused with a clear understanding of its provenance. If the dashboard helps you find a missing identifier, a stale name, an ambiguous institutional boundary, or an outdated DORA or CoARA status, then it is already doing part of its job.
